speaker: interviewer
text: Number eight in the interview with Mrs. Hanna Lifschutz. Hanna, we were talking at the end of tape seven about your sister, about [? Phillips ?] about visit to Curacao.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you see, actually you know, they were hoping-- the Dutch authority, also the Jews [INAUDIBLE] they never wanted to go to Curacao. But the point was to get the transit visa to Japan and then to get hold of a port outside Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they can-- this person took their word. They didn't know themselves. But you know, they hang on any straw. So the world-- the word goes-- so the Jewish people said we can get visas to Curacao provided the Japanese consulate can give us visas, to-- to-- transit visas through Japan. So they went to Japanese consulate. His name was Chiune Sugihara. Really, a man out of this world, you know. I know that the Japanese were cruel to-- very cruel to the English and Americans, but they just didn't overlook the Polish Jews. They didn't have very much in common with them. They overlooked them. They had no-- no-- no business to be-- to be in-- at war with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he didn't ask his authorities, this Chiune, because there were hundreds of Jews already coming to the-- to the premises of the-- of the Japanese consulate. And he and his wife as an act of mercy, he asked but he was set not to grant it. But as an act-- act of mercy taking it upon his own-- him own self, this noble man, very noble man, Chiune Sugihara decided he would stamp the visas for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stamped, stamped, stamped 3,500 visas. He was recalled in protest of doing that. So he knew he was going. So he was still on the station stamping the visas. And then he threw-- apparently that what some people say. I wouldn't say it's true. But the Jews stamped themselves. In this way, 3,500 Jews got visas to Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was recalled. He was put into-- he was in disgrace. And he didn't-- he was I think-- it's-- he had a very-- he was punished. And only now, posthumously, he has been--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rewarded.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not rewarded, but he was pardoned. And the Yad Vashem has called his wife and made her an honorary Gentile and one of the righteous and honored her very greatly with a great ceremony. Now, the Jews, 3,500, had the visas. Now, the Russians declared if you have a visa to Japan, transit visa through Japan to Curacao, you have to pay us $200 and then you can go to trans-Siberian train to Vladivostok, which, you know, it just across from Moscow to across Russia. And then you will be ferried to Japan. And so it was. But there were again, terrible these Russians were, because they were spite. My sister will tell you a story because I don't want any longer to speak. She'll tell her own, personal, very, very, very interesting story. But if they were-- there was a place called Intourist, where you had to register and put the $200. If the Russians have seen you going there, very often they stopped you, took the $200 away. And if-- and for having dollars in Russia they-- you-- they sent you to-- to Siberia. But anyway, my sister and my husband, likewise, managed to get the visa. And they were in batches. In 30, 30, 30, 30. They went. They paid the $200. They went to-- to the-- they-- they obtained places in the train, which was very comfortably nice, and they went to 10 days or more and stopped at each--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Station, [INAUDIBLE] and all this haven for Jews. And on each station they stopped, they saw people ghost-like and Jews and everybody begging to be saved and no-- no food, nothing. All you could get on all these stations, when they went hoping to get something-- because on the train they mostly ate potatoes or whatever they boarded in Russia. In Moskva, that you could get a lot of things still-- cabbages and God knows. You could get ice cream and thermometers. That's what you could get. Anyway, they-- they survived the train. Some people were scared that they are going to be taken to Siberia and to camps, but the Russians kept their word good. And they-- the train brought them to Vladivostok, which was the last point of the Russian Empire-- Soviet Russia. Soviet Russia. And from there they went by rafts, small tug, small tugs, fish boats-- God knows what-- going in, you know, could have been-- could have been, you know, sunk in the sea to Suruga.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Japan. And they went to the-- they saved 3,500 Jews. They went to Suruga. They stayed for six months. And they were received in Suruga by 50 Russian families who lived in Japan. The 50 Russian families, rich Jewish families, they escaped as white Russians from the Soviet Union. They formed the committee-- they escaped long ago. They were very wealthy. They formed a committee and they paid to the Japanese committee. And for the Jewish committee they paid, like, yens, this and this amount of yen and supported 3,500 Jews. Afterwards HIAS, the-- the American HIAS began to send money. And so everybody was-- had this amount of money. They have-- they visited the country. They lived there. The Japanese were extremely good and nice. They had nothing to the Polish Jews. And then slowly but surely, they all went to the Chinese ports, smuggled themselves off Shanghai. And there were enormous vessels going to around the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister and her 30 people-- my sister became great, great friend of my husband, and they knew each other. And then she-- they boarded a-- a-- a boat which went to India. The captain didn't want to know. He knew there is some refugees and didn't demand tickets or anything. So they smuggled themselves and arrived in India. And they had a very hard time in the beginning because before they made-- not before the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- there-- there were some organizations took care of them and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband-- my future husband-- went to Burma in this way. Some people went to Australia. Some people went to all kinds of places and then went to Canada. And that was how these 3,500 refugees saved their life. My sister went to India and then after many hard years and illness and all that, she-- she married actually. And then she sent a visa for me. That was the-- And India, didn't know what was going on in Europe. They had no inkling, no idea. In India, there were many Polish Jews, German Jews, Austrian Jews, Czech Jews, all the Jews-- all the Jews from all over Europe. And they had no idea what-- how devastated Europe. And my sister was praying somebody should be left of the family and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came.

speaker: interviewee
text: God Almighty and providence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to India?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to India.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it? When was it? What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: May-- after all this journey from the-- the-- May 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister has provided everything for me and a hotel and-- and beautiful clothes. And, you know, it was like-- heaven on Earth is not a comparison. She took great care of me and she was wonderful to me and also my this brother-in-law was very nice. And then after one year, I met my husband who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: He came back to India?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came through various-- came to India from Rangoon. And he was a very, very, very clever man. I was extremely lucky to have met him. He was clever. He was good to me and we married after a year. He was a very skilled man in-- he was trained in glass-- in-- in-- in making glass, a glass engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where he was originally from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from-- he was born in Lwow. And he was already then as a young man-- he was trained to be an expert in glass making. And then he built--

speaker: interviewer
text: In India?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few factories--

speaker: interviewer
text: In India?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Indian government slowly sent all the refugees away, but he was asked to stay because he made a terrific contribution to the glass industry in India, building in Hyderabad. They building in Bombay glass factories and employing lot of people. And I have all kinds of documents and things of his work. He-- and I-- I was blessed for-- for-- for having met him and the children were born there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Vivian and Alex. And I was given, you know, I-- I-- so many most magnificent people died, wonderful people of all distinction, and I was just, you know, a girl with no visible contribution to the world. My only explanation is that in this face of this terrible adversity, we did survive. We married. We had children. We educated children. I'm so proud of my children. My son is very, very already quite prominent architect. My daughter-in-law is a writer. My daughter is a-- is a-- is-- is a interior decorator, and my son-in-law is a doctor, dentist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I managed to educate them. I managed to provide-- to-- to-- to-- to-- to make them good citizens and humble people and to be modest and kind to others. And that is my contribution and I'm grateful for it. I'm never-- actually, when people say are you proud? No, no, it is a wrong expression. I am not proud. I am most grateful. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am most grateful to have been able to-- to survive this all and-- and have the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: As it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You know, for that I am grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in India after [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in India practically 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 16 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the children still fairly small, I came over here. It was not only-- I liked the Indian people immensely. Were very good people, very nice people, very helpful people. I really, truly got a great affiliation with the Indian people-- Muslims-- Muslim and-- and Hindu alike. Even more with Muslims because our religion was very similar. But I came because health-wise. I was-- I couldn't stand the climate. I was very ill. I had to undergo few operations, [INAUDIBLE] operations. I had suffered from amoebic dysentery. And eventually the doctors said that if you will not leave India for a year at least and go to a cool climate, you will be-- you-- you-- you will not survive it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1959-- '58, '59-- my husband said, look, you go first. I promise you-- he was building the last factory, which was [INAUDIBLE] already. I will build it-- and it was quite a exceptional factory provided with all facilities for the workers. He did a lot of the poor-- he liked the poor people of India much more than the rich. And I'll finish this and I'll come to join you. And you go to England. I went to Israel-- Why to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Israel first. I went to Israel, but my health didn't permit me. I had to go to a climate for one year to get rid-- I had to go to the hospital of tropical disease. I was ill and Alex, my son, and my daughter all-- we all had amoebic dysentery. It was very serious. But after a year, I took them to schools and then my husband had-- couldn't be-- India didn't recognize Israel. Our money couldn't be transferred to India because he sold the factory. Could be only to a Anglo-Saxon country. And so eventually I settled.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ended up here in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: In England, yeah. That-- that is how I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming now to the end of the interview. Do you like to add anything that you forgot to mention? Anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I actually don't want-- I-- I regret, I regret most deeply because I heard from my aunt who was in the great ghetto after that she met once my little brother Michael. She met him once, but he obviously perished. And poor Rosa perished and my father perished and my auntie, beloved Auntie Lutka. And I-- it's sort of aberration. It's-- you know, it's in my mind and in my heart that I was not able to save any of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that is my regret, my deep regret. But it was-- it was not easy. It was-- I don't make excuses, but it-- it was not easy. I couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I want to add.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any message you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only want to-- I want to say that, oh-- the two years to 1992, I finished my-- the-- the-- the-- I-- I have written my-- my manuscript about all what I had-- my-- my daughter-in-law who-- who is a writer in her own right, she wrote three books and she writes scripts for BBC and so on. One day when she has time and time because she needs that, then she can make the-- not only revise the book but edit the book properly. And perhaps not in my lifetime anymore, but because I was in a hurry and I am not young, so I sent it to Yad Vashem and to the Polish Historical Institute in Warsaw to-- not knowing about the Spielberg foundation, that some evidence should already exist through my life, through my writing. What can I-- I'm grateful that Mr. Spielberg is so generous that she-- that he-- that he-- that-- that he organized this foundation, that our-- our testimony will be available for-- for-- I hope to God. And I hope that things like that which happened to the Jewish people and which he, Hitler, had first started with Jewish people and then would end with anybody else. That unfortunately is happening, what happened in Bosnia and now in Rwanda. That, how on Earth-- when we will learn the lesson that we should not do these things to human beings. It seems like Hitler has spread a poison like a Pandora box, which can never be closed. And my wish is that people should be tolerant to each other and forgive, forget. Can't forgive. Can't forget. Can't forget but forgive and live somehow in peace with a new generation of people who have nothing to do with it. And not keep the anger in the heart, but at the same time, never forget it and teach other people, another generation a lesson. That-- that this is what-- what should be remembered. And I'm grateful to you for being so-- so very kind and sympathetic and looking at your pretty face, which makes my story easier to say. And to dear James who has all the patience listening to the stories, which must be terrific to you, very hard. Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wish you all the best, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm very pleased about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, Hanna, would you like to present your two lovely grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed, they are my two lovely grandchildren. This is the children of Monique and Alex, my son Alex. And this is Sophie, and she's 13 years old. She is an adorable girl, an adorable girl. Absolutely gorgeous, lovely character, sweet and kind, adores her-- adores her-- her parents and adores her brother and is very sweet to me. And she learns-- she's a very good pupil. She learns very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can I say about Sophie? She draws lovely, and she's always helpful in everything. And she always wanted to have a dog, very much so. But because of the circumstances, we can't have a dog, so she has two guinea pigs. Now Sophie can say something? And then-- and then I present-- then I present Jonathan, Jonathan Simon. And we call him Pon. And I ask my daughter-in-law, Monique, I said, why do you call him Pon at the beginning? She said because Jonathan, Ponathon, Pon. So he's Pon. We call him Pon, Ponny

speaker: interviewee
text: And he is a young man who goes to [INAUDIBLE] school. He is-- he's very bright, very entertaining. Very little patience, he has. And he is very good at school and he-- his hobby's chess, and he won recently a silver medal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: For chess. Out of six games, he won five. And he's very sweet. He comes from time to time here, and we entertain him with some nice [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] food, sort of whatever comes along. And he plays card. I'm teaching him how to play rummy. And I'm very proud to have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grateful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud and very, very-- as I said before-- mostly grateful to have such gorgeous grandchildren produced by the couple. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Sophie, did you ever heard your grandma's story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I read it in my Sunday school because we're learning about the Second World War, so we read this as part of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a bit young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you will hear about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably. I want to hear about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, perhaps on the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It'd be easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you have other grandchildren, Hanna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have-- the-- the eldest is Matthiew. You met him this morning-- this afternoon rather. Very, very, very nice, sweet-natured boy. And then you met Jessica, and she's also at the university. She's learning-- she has taken graphics. And there is also Simon, Simon Jonathan. He's Jonathan Simon, and the other one is Simon Jonathan. And he's a young man of 16. And we have some difficulties sometimes in persuading him to come, for example. But otherwise, he's-- he's lovely. He's lovely and we all love-- each one-- each one dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's the-- they're all the children of--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's still at school. He's-- Simon is still at school. Very, very good at computer, very good in mathematics, very, very, very good at all these things. He's still at school. The others, but you will-- will-- will study further, I hope, for his PhD. And Jessica is at Nottingham University. And that's all these other children of-- of Vivian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vivian, your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vivian my daughter, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. There anything you'd like to add? And-- and-- unfortunately you don't meet my son, but he's lovely too. I believe you. OK, anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie? Would you like to say something, my heart? Sophie's very good. Sophie wants to learn drama. Now, you say something about it then. She's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be an actress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actress? Like theater actress? Television?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's directing a little play at school, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm directing at my school for a drama competition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? And when is it going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just had finals today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, that's great, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: How was it going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? And Ponny's also good at football. Oh, tell us something. How do you play? How do you know how to play this-- this-- this difficult game, Ponny? How? Tell this young lady how.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the moves, how did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you learn it in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I learnt it mostly at home. Then I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy started it, hasn't she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she started, and then Daddy gave me some help. And then I went to the chess club because I was really keen. And-- and I-- and I'm-- I'm getting private lessons at home and things like that. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: You like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You like it. That's wonderful. Great. OK, it was very nice to know you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you.
